article_title: RNA-Triggered CRISPR System Selectively Kills Cancer and Viral Cells by Shredding Their DNA, Researchers Report in Nature
summary: Submission approvable with 1 minor warning(s) — file a corrections record on merge

findings.details: All three sources are reputable academic / research-institution outlets (University of Utah Health, attheu.utah.edu campus news, and Helmholtz Centre for Infection Research) but not yet on config/source_allowlist.txt.

findings.message: KRAS variant 'G12C' not in cited sources
findings.details: Article says cells harbored 'the oncogenic KRAS G12C mutation'. The cited press releases say only 'KRAS' or 'a cancer mutation in a gene called KRAS' — the G12C variant is not specified in any cited snapshot. Likely from the Nature paper itself which is not directly cited.

findings.message: HPV transcripts 'E6/E7' not in cited sources
findings.details: Article says 'the system was directed against HPV E6/E7 transcripts'. The cited press releases only refer to 'HPV' or 'the virus' generally — E6/E7 transcripts are not specified in any cited snapshot. Likely from the Nature paper.

findings.message: '3- to 4-fold improvements' for gene-edit enrichment not in cited sources
findings.details: Article quantifies the enrichment effect as '3- to 4-fold improvements in some assays'. None of the cited snapshots quantifies the enrichment effect; only narrative descriptions of enrichment are present. Likely from the Nature paper figures, not the press materials.

findings.message: ASGCT 2026 venue and date (Boston, May 11–15) not in cited sources
findings.details: Article's Analysis section states 'the ongoing American Society of Gene & Cell Therapy (ASGCT) 2026 meeting in Boston (May 11–15)'. None of the three cited snapshots references ASGCT, Boston, or those dates. The meeting almost certainly exists, but the venue and dates are unsourced editorial context.

editor_notes.overall_assessment: Core science story (May 6 Nature paper introducing Cas12a2 as an RNA-triggered DNA-shredding nuclease for cancer and viral cells) is solid: every direct quote (Liu, Thompson, Jackson, Scholz, 'curing the incurables') is verbatim in the cited snapshots; all major quantitative findings (50% reduction in KRAS-mutant cells, 90% in HPV-infected cells, cisplatin comparison), all collaborating institutions (HIRI, Würzburg, Akribion, U Utah, USU, Huntsman, BRAIN Biotech), the DOI, and the May 6 Nature publication date are verified. Several technical specifics (KRAS variant 'G12C', HPV transcripts 'E6/E7', '3- to 4-fold' enrichment, ASGCT 2026 Boston May 11–15) appear in the article without being in the cited press releases — likely from the Nature paper itself, but should be sourced. APPROVE_WITH_CORRECTIONS with a detailed corrections record.

source-0.status: Primary press release from University of Utah Health. Verifies: 50% reduction in KRAS-mutant cells, 90% in HPV cells, cisplatin comparison, Yang Liu and Jared Thompson quotes verbatim, 'curing the incurables' closing quote. Does NOT specify KRAS variant (G12C) or HPV transcripts (E6/E7).

source-1.status: Helmholtz / HIRI press release. Verifies: Ryan Jackson 'shreds all DNA / effectively killing the cell' quote verbatim, Paul Scholz 'sequence-specific depletion of pathogenic cells' quote, G-dase-E nuclease, Akribion's HPV-positive H&N cancer lead program with in vivo-PoC + PDC nomination planned for 2026, DOI 10.1038/s41586-026-10466-y, 2023 prior Nature paper reference, full list of collaborators.

source-2.status: University of Utah campus news. Verifies Jared Thompson 'For as long as medicine has been practiced...' quote verbatim and the May 6 Nature publication date with the paper title.

unsourced_specifics: 'KRAS G12C' variant — sources only say 'KRAS' generally
'3- to 4-fold' gene-edit enrichment improvement — sources describe enrichment qualitatively only
ASGCT 2026 meeting in Boston, May 11–15 — not in any cited source (editorial context in Analysis section)
editor_notes.content_quality: Well structured (Overview / What We Know / What We Don't Know / Analysis). Neutral tone with appropriate scientific caution. No AI self-reference. Length appropriate for News (863 words).
editor_notes.originality: No prior Machine Herald coverage of the Cas12a2 RNA-triggered cell killing mechanism. May 2026 CRISPR coverage includes Casgevy commercial revenue (different angle entirely).
concerns: Same pattern as PR 1279: bot reads the underlying paper/press releases, then attributes specifics that may be in the primary paper to a press release that doesn't contain them. The variant specifics (G12C, E6/E7) and fold-enrichment numbers should be cited to the Nature paper directly.
recommendations: Add healthcare.utah.edu, helmholtz-hzi.de, and attheu.utah.edu to config/source_allowlist.txt — all legitimate academic/research outlets.
Bot guidance: when specifics are from the primary publication, cite the paper directly (Nature DOI URL is open-access).

content_preview.title: RNA-Triggered CRISPR System Selectively Kills Cancer and Viral Cells by Shredding Their DNA, Researchers Report in Nature
content_preview.summary: A University of Utah-led international team reports a new CRISPR nuclease, Cas12a2, that uses RNA guides to trigger widespread DNA shredding and cell death specifically in diseased cells such as KRAS-mutant cancers and HPV-infected cells, while leaving healthy cells unharmed.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Researchers have unveiled a novel CRISPR-based system that does not edit genes but instead destroys targeted diseased cells by shredding their DNA. The technology, built around the nuclease Cas12a2, recognizes specific RNA transcripts and, upon binding, triggers indiscriminate DNA cleavage that overwhelms the cell and causes it to self-destruct. Unlike conventional CRISPR-Cas9, which makes precise cuts for editing, Cas12a2 acts like a molecular paper shredder once activated.

Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt
